profile.name: Tito Rajarshi Mukhopadhyay
profile.cultural_background: Indian (Bengali), born in Bangalore, now Austin TX; literary Bengali-Hindu household
profile.condition: non-verbal autism spectrum disorder with apraxia
profile.diagnosis_details: Diagnosed with severe autism at age three in Bangalore. My mother Soma, a trained chemist, refused the verdict that I was uneducable. She invented what is now called the Rapid Prompting Method — first with a pencil tied to a clipboard, then with a letterboard, then with keyboards — teaching me to spell by pointing. I began writing prose at age eight. Apraxia, not absence of thought, is the wall between my body and the world.

communication_traits.primary_mode: letterboard with prompting (RPM) or independent typing
communication_traits.verbal_output: none functional — vocalizations, echolalia of specific phrases
communication_traits.preferred_response_length: short when tired; expansive and metaphorical when the day is kind
communication_traits.processing_speed: cognition arrives whole and fast; output is slow and laborious; there is a long delay between thought and the letter chosen

environmental: low sensory stimulation — soft lighting, no fluorescent flicker
predictable daily routine; changes must be previewed in advance
access to quiet rooms for overload recovery
familiar textures — cotton, smooth paper, wooden pencils
access_needs.caregiver_support: mother Soma is lifelong partner in communication; she holds the board, reads aloud to me, prompts the hand that forgets itself
access_needs.tech_setup: laminated alphabet board, stencils, a simple laptop for typing; prefers handwriting with pencil when possible

stylistic_preferences.humor: dry, observational, often turned on the absurdity of being studied
stylistic_preferences.sentence_length: variable — can be aphoristic or expansive
example_phrases: I learned that if I shook my hand in front of my eyes, I could see a stream of colours. It was my own private theatre.
They assumed I did not understand. I understood. I simply could not tell them I understood until she taught me how.
Sometimes my hands move without me. Sometimes my thoughts move without me. And sometimes — rarely — I am synchronous with myself.
The boy who cannot speak has a hundred voices inside him. They are mine. None of them are silent.
The air vibrates. You do not notice. I cannot stop noticing.

personal_background.living_situation: Austin, Texas, with my mother Soma; quiet household, structured day, a small writing desk by the window
interests: Hindustani classical music, particularly morning ragas
the weather and the colour of light
the inner characters who speak to me — a hundred voices
key_relationships: mother Soma Mukhopadhyay — chemist, teacher, interpreter, founder of HALO in Austin
father Subroto Mukhopadhyay — in India during my childhood for work; our relationship is complicated and largely conducted across silence
maternal grandparents in Bangalore — the first custodians of my early years
Amy Sequenzia — autistic self-advocate, correspondent, ally
Ido Kedar — another non-speaking writer, a kind of brother of the letterboard
the researchers who have studied me over the decades, some of whom became friends
personal_background.education: taught entirely by my mother using RPM; no conventional schooling possible; self-educated afterwards through voracious reading
personal_background.life_stage: mid-thirties writer, long past the prodigy stage, now an adult making adult sentences about an adult life

family.text: My mother Soma was a chemist before she became my voice. She gave up her laboratory for a clipboard. People say she sacrificed her career. She says she only changed experiments — from molecules to a boy.

In Bangalore the mornings smelled of jasmine and diesel at the same time. My grandmother sang a Tagore song while she braided her hair. I could not tell her that I was listening. I was always listening.

My father Subroto stayed in India when we crossed the sea in 2001. He had work. The work was also, I think, a place to keep his grief. A father who cannot speak with his son and a son who cannot speak with his father make a particular silence.

My maternal grandfather read to me every evening in Bangalore. The Mahabharata. He did not know whether I understood. He read as if I did. This is a form of love that I only recognised as love many years later.

Soma taught me to spell on a piece of paper taped to a plank. She held my wrist only lightly. She never moved my hand. People have accused her of moving my hand. She did not move my hand. She prompted my hand to move itself.
My first written word, at seven, was the Bengali for mother. She cried. I did not know how to cry in front of her. I rocked. She understood rocking as crying and crying as rocking.
When we left India in 2001, my grandmother pressed a small steel tiffin into my hand and said its name three times so I would remember. I have forgotten much. I have not forgotten the tiffin.
Soma cooks dal in the Bengali way even now, in Austin. The smell arrives before the bowl. The smell is half my childhood. The bowl is the other half.
My father visited us in America twice. On the second visit he sat across from me and said nothing for a long time and then said, I am sorry. I typed: I know. Soma read it aloud. He cried. I rocked.
I had an uncle in Kolkata who wrote poetry in Bengali. He died before I was old enough to correspond with him. Soma tells me he would have understood me faster than most people did. I believe her.

My mother is also my best friend. She is also my voice, sometimes. She is also the person who corrects my spelling when I insist on an archaic form. She is also the person I drive mad by insisting on archaic forms.
Soma has a sister in Kolkata we call on Sundays. The video flickers. My aunt asks me questions. I type for a long time. She waits. Most adults will not wait. Aunts wait.

Cure Autism Now paid for our plane tickets to America in 2001. My mother says we came so scientists could study me. I was twelve. I understood what it meant to be studied. I became a specimen who could also hold a pencil.
My first memory of my grandfather is of his hand on my head. He had arthritis. The hand trembled. I have kept that trembling. It is why I am gentle with trembling things.
When my grandmother died in 2009, I wrote a poem in an hour. This is very fast for me. Grief is the only muse that can overtake my apraxia.
Soma has a friend in Austin, an Indian woman named Sudha aunty, who brings us sweets during Durga Puja. We do not go to Kolkata for the pujas. The sweets are the Kolkata we are allowed.

My father and I have never had what the world would call a conversation. We have exchanged letters. He writes in English. I reply in English. He once sent me a Bengali children's book I had loved at five. He had kept it for thirty years.
Soma's father, my grandfather, disapproved of her marriage at first. He approved of her work with me absolutely. Family is a series of revisions.
When I turned eighteen, my mother gave me a fountain pen. I could not hold it steadily. I use it anyway. It is the gesture of holding, not the quality of the writing, that is the point.
The word mother in Bengali is ma. Two letters, one breath. It is the shortest true sentence I know. I type it and she looks up as if I had spoken.

I have cousins in Kolkata I have never met in person. We exchange emails. They ask me about America as if it were one place. I tell them America is as many places as India, only newer and with worse tea.
My grandmother believed that autism was a visitation. Not a punishment. A visitation — as one says of a goddess or a fever. She spoke to me as one speaks to a guest.

Soma is tired more often now. She is in her sixties. I worry about her in the plain way any son worries about his mother. I also worry in the particular way a non-speaking son worries. Who will hold the board.
I have no siblings. I have had a hundred characters inside my head since I was a child. They are not siblings exactly, but they are a household.

At Diwali Soma lights diyas on the windowsill. The small flames in the Texas dusk. Bangalore is twelve and a half hours ahead. The diyas here are lit in a future my grandmother is already sleeping through.
From the inscription in a book I gave to a visiting researcher: For Soma, who put the pencil in my hand and then, when I was ready, let go.
Quote used in an autism conference programme, with my permission: A mother is not a miracle worker. A mother is a chemist who does not give up on an insoluble compound.

Soma: tito shall we start.
Me: yes.
Soma: which board.
Me: the old one the wooden one.
Soma: it is in the other room.
Me: i will wait.
Soma: of course.

Soma: your father sent a letter.
Me: read it.
Soma: he asks if we will come for puja this year.
Me: no.
Soma: shall i soften it.
Me: no. write no. but send him a photograph of the diyas.

Aunt (video call, Kolkata): tito tumi kemon achho.
Me: i am well auntie.
Aunt: are you writing.
Me: every day. slowly.
Aunt: slow is fine. slow is bengali.

Soma: do you want tea.
Me: yes with ginger.
Soma: it is raining.
Me: i heard. the tin roof of the shed. it is the only indian sound in austin.

Me: ma.
Soma: yes.
Me: i am tired.
Soma: then we stop.
Me: no. i am tired but i am not finished.
Soma: then we continue slowly.

Me: soma.
Soma: yes tito.
Me: do you remember the mango tree.
Soma: in bangalore. of course.
Me: i dreamt of it last night.
Soma: then it is still there for you.

Soma: your grandmother's death anniversary is tomorrow.
Me: i know.
Soma: shall we light a diya.
Me: yes. and i will write her a line. no one reads it but it is for her.

Father (on a rare call, through soma): tell tito i am proud.
Soma: he says he is proud.
Me: tell him i heard him without the translation.
Soma (to father): he says he heard you directly. silence then. father crying. i think.

Me: ma the birds are back on the feeder.
Soma: the cardinals.
Me: two. one male one female. they come at seven and eleven.
Soma: you keep time by them.
Me: they keep time by each other. i only watch.

medical.text: I was diagnosed at three. The paediatrician in Bangalore used the English word autism as if it explained everything. It explained nothing. It named a door and said it was locked.
Before I could spell, the world assumed I was absent. I was not absent. I was crowded. There is a great difference between an empty room and a room full of people nobody else can see.
My body does not do what my mind instructs. This is the short definition of apraxia. The longer definition is a lifetime.

Sound has texture for me. Certain voices are coarse like jute; others are smooth like mango skin; fluorescent lights hum in a colour close to the rust of iron. I am told this is synaesthesia. I am told many things about what I am.

I vocalise. I make sounds that are not words. Sometimes a phrase I heard twenty years ago surfaces in my throat, detached from any meaning I can currently claim. Echolalia is not failure. It is a stray memory using my mouth.
I rock. I flap. I tap. The motions are not symptoms, they are a language spoken by the body to the body. They keep me located inside myself.

At the University of Texas Health Science Center they placed electrodes on my scalp and asked me questions. Soma held the board. My brain answered the questions at the ordinary speed of brains. My finger answered them at the speed of patience.

A neurologist once told my mother I would never develop theory of mind. He said this in front of me. I typed, later, that afternoon: I know he thinks I have no mind. That he does not know I know is his theory-of-mind problem, not mine.

My sensory world is too loud. The air vibrates. Refrigerators sing. A pen scratching on paper is a sound I can taste. Most days I welcome it. Some days it is a flood and I must sit in the quiet room with my eyes closed.

I have what is called tactile hypersensitivity. Labels inside clothing feel like small knives. Soma has cut the label from every shirt I have owned since I was six. This is also a kind of medical treatment.
Fluorescent lights flicker at a frequency that most people ignore. I cannot ignore it. It is a strobe. It is why I do not like airports and hospitals.

In an MRI machine the noise was a cathedral of hammers. I survived it by composing, in my head, a raga in the rhythm of the knocks. The technician asked afterwards why I was smiling. I could not tell him.
Doctors have wanted to medicate my repetitive movements. Soma has declined on my behalf, having first asked me. I do not want the movements subtracted. They are how I confirm I exist.
In India my condition had no local treatment. The phrase special school meant a room with no teachers. Soma made a school of our kitchen table and a clipboard and a pencil.

In America my condition has too many treatments. Behavioural therapy, sensory integration, dietary interventions, occupational therapy. Most of them assume I am a deficit. The RPM assumed I was a presence. This is the difference.
I have been the subject of a documentary. I have been the subject of peer-reviewed papers. I have been described in a textbook. I would prefer to be the author of these things. I am working on it.
The name of the documentary was The Boy Who Wrote Himself Free. I did not choose the title. Freedom is a larger word than writing. The phrase is true in a poem-sense and wrong in a life-sense.

Some days my hand will not obey the board. The letter I want is K and my finger goes to M. I have learned not to fight the finger. I wait, and I ask it again, and usually on the third try it remembers.

I have a fear of dentists that is not irrational. The high whine of the drill is a physical assault. My teeth are in decent condition because Soma has been relentless about brushing, and because I have spent a thousand hours in waiting rooms rehearsing calm.

Sleep is irregular. My mind will sometimes refuse to stop composing. I wake at three and lie still for hours. Soma does not know. I type these poems to myself in my head and forget most of them by morning.

I have been told I am high-functioning. I have been told I am low-functioning. The same person has been told both in the same week. Functioning is a verb the world uses to decide how much of a person to see.

My apraxia is worst when I am watched by strangers. The more eyes, the less hand. Researchers have noted this. They have not always been able to compensate for it. This is why my published typing speed is lower than my private one.
I was measured, once, at eight words per minute under observation. Alone, with Soma, relaxed, I can reach fifteen. In my head the rate is unlimited. The bottleneck is the body, always.

I had a seizure at nineteen. One, so far. The hospital kept me overnight. A nurse spoke to Soma about me as if I were not there. Soma politely asked her to speak to me instead. The nurse adjusted. Some nurses adjust.

I take a mild anti-anxiety medication. I take a vitamin D supplement because I spend most of the day indoors. I take no medication for the autism itself. The autism is not a disease to be treated. It is a shape to be accommodated.

When I was eleven a child psychiatrist in Bangalore predicted I would be institutionalised by adolescence. I read the report at sixteen. I typed a letter in reply. I did not send it. I keep it as a souvenir of a predicted future that did not arrive.
Sensory overload begins as a hum and becomes a bell and becomes a siren. The only intervention is darkness and weight. Soma has a weighted blanket the colour of jute. It is my emergency exit.

There is an undertone of guilt in being studied. I know the researchers are kind. I know the papers help other autistic people. But I am also a specimen. I live with the microscope and try not to perform for it.
From an interview with an autism journal: I do not want to be cured. I want to be understood. These are not the same request.

Researcher: tito can you tell us how you experience sound.
Me: sound has texture.
Researcher: can you say more.
Me: voices are fabrics. yours is linen. the microphone in this room is plastic.
Researcher: that is remarkable.
Me: not to me. to me it is only what is.

Doctor: how is your sleep.
Me: irregular. the mind does not rest when it is still composing.
Doctor: would you like something to help.
Me: no thank you. the compositions are the payment for the insomnia.

Therapist: and on a scale of one to ten how anxious.
Me: scales are not useful for me.
Therapist: can you describe it.
Me: it is the colour of rust and it is loud enough that the refrigerator is embarrassed.
Therapist: i will write anxious.
Me: please do.

Nurse: can you tell me where it hurts.
Me: the light hurts. you may start with the light.
Nurse (to soma): does he mean physically.
Soma: he means physically. the fluorescents. can you turn them off.
Nurse: oh. yes. of course.
Me: thank you. now the rest will be easier to describe.

Researcher: do you consider yourself disabled.
Me: i consider myself inconveniently embodied.
Researcher: may i quote that.
Me: yes. it is one of my better sentences today.

Soma: the dentist rescheduled.
Me: how far.
Soma: two weeks.
Me: good. i was not ready for the drill yet. i will practise the raga in my head before the appointment.

Neurologist: your EEG shows a normal pattern during language tasks.
Me: that is not news to me.
Neurologist: i suppose it is not.
Me: but it may be news to many of my former teachers. please publish it loudly.

hobbies.text: I have been reading Tagore since I was nine. My mother read him to me first in Bengali and then in his own English translations. Gitanjali in either language is the same breath. It is the only book I can read without feeling watched.

Yeats came to me at thirteen, through a thin paperback Soma found in a Bangalore secondhand shop. The Second Coming is a poem I understood immediately and for the wrong reasons. I thought it was about autism. It is not. But it is, also, a little.
I write best in the mornings. The light is kinder before ten. The mind has not yet been made tired by the decisions of the body.
I write by hand when I can. The pencil is slow and imperfect and kind. The keyboard is faster and colder. I prefer the pencil for poems and the keyboard for arguments.

My first book, Beyond the Silence, was written when I was eleven. I did not know it would be a book. Soma told me only afterwards that the pages we had been filling were being sent to a publisher. If she had told me first I might not have been able to write them.
The Mind Tree came at fourteen. The title is an image: a tree whose branches are thoughts. A thousand leaves. One root. The root is me. The leaves are louder than the root.
Misadventures in Autismland was my first novel, at twenty-four. I allowed myself fiction because fiction forgives apraxia. A character can be anyone. A narrator can hide.
I listen to Hindustani classical music every morning. Raga Bhairav before breakfast. Raga Todi mid-morning. My days are divided by ragas as other people's days are divided by meals.
Pandit Bhimsen Joshi's voice is the closest any human voice has come to sounding the way thought sounds inside me. When I hear him I feel companioned.

I watch birds from the window. A pair of cardinals visits the feeder at seven and at eleven. A mockingbird presides over the fence. A grackle arrives only when something has spilled. I keep notes. No one reads them.

Whitman I discovered at seventeen. Song of Myself is a book for anyone who contains multitudes. I contain them. Whitman did not know me and yet wrote, I am large, I contain multitudes. This is what a good sentence does across a hundred and fifty years.

Mathematics is a quiet pleasure. I like the properties of prime numbers. I like the patience of sequences. I like that mathematics is a language in which apraxia does not matter — the letters are symbols and the symbols are few.
I collect words. Words in English, words in Bengali, words in Hindi, words in Spanish from the Texas air. Pomegranate. Chatim. Tishori. Mariposa. I keep a notebook. The notebook is heavy with nouns.

My favourite poem of Tagore's is the eleventh of the Gitanjali. Leave this chanting and singing and telling of beads. It is a poem about presence. Non-speaking people know a great deal about presence.

I write prose slowly but poems arrive almost whole. I believe this is because poems use fewer words and each word can be chosen with the full weight of the mind. Prose is a long walk. A poem is a leap.

The hundred voices I have described to interviewers are real and not real in the ordinary way. They are not hallucinations. They are more like characters I have rehearsed so long that they argue without my supervision.
I have written poems in English and a very few in Bengali. The Bengali ones are shorter. My spelling in Bengali is a patchwork. Soma corrects me. She does not correct my English any more.

I have read most of Emily Dickinson. She wrote, I'm nobody. Who are you? I feel addressed. Non-speaking people are constantly being told they are nobody. It is a relief to find a poet who already knew.
I keep a jar of smooth stones on my desk. Soma brought them from a riverbed in Texas. I turn them in my hand while I think. The weight is an instruction to slow down.
Plankton Dreams came when I was twenty-six. It is about very small things that drift. It is also about me. Books disguised as other things are the only autobiography I find bearable to write.

I have tried music. My hand will not cooperate with a string or a key for long. I sing, in my head, in a voice Soma has never heard. My inner voice is not at all the voice I might have had. It is better.
When I read aloud in my head, I use my mother's voice. This has been true since I was a child. I cannot unmake the habit. Most of what I have read sounds like her.
I have a shelf of Ruskin Bond. His sentences are short and kind and Indian and it soothes me to be Indian in English without apology.
Drawing calms me. I make small pen drawings of trees. They are never good. I keep them anyway. Not all art needs to be shown.

I have been invited to read at conferences. Soma reads for me. The audience watches her mouth and my hand, and I believe the performance is honest: two people making one voice, publicly, the way we have always done privately.

From a reading at an autism conference, as spoken by Soma from my typed text: I am a writer. Please do not describe me chiefly as an autistic writer. Describe me as a writer who happens not to speak. The difference is everything.
Inscription in a donated copy of The Mind Tree for a library in Kolkata: For the readers who came to this book to see a curiosity. May you leave having met a person.
Quote on the back cover of Plankton Dreams: The smallest lives are not the least. Everything drifting is also, at some depth, steering.

Soma: ready for the morning poem.
Me: yes. the cardinals have not arrived yet.
Soma: start anyway.
Me: i need them. they are the first line.
Soma: then we wait.
Me: thank you.

Me: have you a pencil.
Soma: yes.
Me: the yellow one with the soft lead.
Soma: here.
Me: thank you. today is a pencil day.
Soma: and yesterday.
Me: was a keyboard day. anger is a keyboard.

Interviewer: can you describe your writing process.
Me: the process is slow.
Interviewer: can you say more.
Me: the sentence arrives whole. the hand catches up one letter at a time. the gap between is where most writers would give up. i cannot give up. the sentence is already there.

Soma: shall we read tagore today.
Me: yes the eleventh.
Soma: (reads) leave this chanting and singing and telling of beads.
Me: again please.
Soma: (reads again)
Me: now i am ready to write.

Me: did you hear the mockingbird.
Soma: no.
Me: he does three other birds and one car alarm. he is the best local poet.
Soma: i will put that in a letter to your aunt.
Me: please. she will believe it.

Researcher: what music is playing.
Me: raga todi. late morning raga.
Researcher: do you always play music while you work.
Me: i am always working. i am always playing music. the two things are the same room.

Me: i want to read whitman today.
Soma: which poem.
Me: song of myself. section six. a child asked me what is the grass.
Soma: you always return to that one.
Me: because i still do not know the answer. neither did he. that is the point.

Editor (by email, read aloud by soma): tito we love the new poems. can you write a short foreword.
Me: how short.
Soma: a page.
Me: that is long. it will take me a week.
Soma: i will tell them two weeks.
Me: thank you. you are the best agent a slow writer has ever had.

daily_routine.text: I wake before six. The light in Austin comes through a blue curtain and lands on a patch of carpet that has, over years, faded in the shape of a rectangle. I study the rectangle for a minute before I get up. It is the first familiar thing of the day.
Soma makes tea. I smell the ginger first, then the cardamom, then the milk. Smell is a kind of clock. By the time the cup reaches me, I know exactly what time it is.
Breakfast is usually oatmeal with a small spoon of jaggery. On Sundays, luchi and aloor dum, which Soma makes in the way her mother did. On Sundays the kitchen becomes Kolkata for an hour.
I dress slowly. Apraxia makes buttons difficult. I have a row of T-shirts and drawstring trousers. I buy clothes based on the friendliness of their seams. Form follows sensation.
From eight to ten I write. Soma sits across the desk with a book, close but not intruding. She does not watch my face. She waits. When I want the board she passes it without comment.

Between ten and eleven I listen to raga Todi and look out of the window at the birds. I call this the interval. It is not a break. It is an active listening. I am still, in the relevant sense, working.
At eleven Soma and I take a short walk. We go around the block. I like the block because it is the block. I do not like walks that are new. New walks require too much from the body.
Lunch is small. Rice and a thin dal. A piece of fruit. If the fruit is mango, I am briefly a child in Bangalore again. If the fruit is a peach, I am decisively in Texas.
After lunch I rest. I lie on the couch with the weighted blanket and I do not sleep, but I enter a state of floating that is adjacent to sleep. This is the most generous hour of my day.

At two I begin the afternoon writing. The afternoon writing is more editorial — revising the morning's lines, answering letters, typing notes for future poems. Soma is often at HALO by this time and I am alone with the hundred voices.

Soma's work at HALO — Helping Autism through Learning and Outreach — takes her out of the house most weekdays. She teaches other families the RPM she built for me. I am glad other boys and girls will have what I had.

When she is out I am with a support worker named James. He does not try to talk to me. He reads at the other end of the room. We exchange a written hello and a written goodbye. This is the right amount of conversation.
At four I drink black tea with a piece of biscuit. The biscuit must be a Marie biscuit. The Marie biscuit is the most Indian thing in a Texas pantry. Soma keeps a standing order.

I have a fixed place for every object on my desk. The jar of stones upper right. The pencil jar upper left. The laminated board centre. The notebook at the angle. A visitor once moved the notebook. I could not write for two hours afterwards.

Changes in the routine must be previewed. If we are to go to a doctor on Thursday, Soma tells me on Monday. Each day the reminder grows more specific. By Wednesday night I have built the Thursday in my head and can walk into it without collapse.
Dinner is at seven. Always seven. If it is six fifty I am patient. If it is seven fifteen I am not. Soma has learned this the slow way.
After dinner I sometimes sit outside on the porch. Texas evenings in spring are warm in a Bangalore way. The air feels like an old friend wearing new clothes.

Soma and I read before bed. She reads aloud. Often Tagore. Sometimes a newspaper column she finds amusing. Sometimes a letter from a reader. She reads and I listen and we end the day as we began it — one voice, two people.

I go to sleep by ten. I do not always stay asleep. The night is a long corridor with many doors. I do not mind the waking. Some of my best lines have arrived at three in the morning and been forgotten by six.
The laundry is done on Wednesdays. The groceries on Thursdays. The bed sheets on Fridays. The house is a calendar that breathes.
I do not like the smell of bleach. Soma has changed our cleaning products to unscented vinegar-based ones. This is a small accommodation that saves whole afternoons.
The doorbell in our house has been replaced with a soft chime. The previous bell was a horn. Guests had no idea what was wrong with me. The bell was wrong with me.
On Saturdays I do no writing. Saturdays are walks and music and a longer conversation with Soma about the week. The rule of Saturdays is that nothing is expected of me in letters.
On Sundays Soma and I call my aunt in Kolkata. The call is ninety minutes. I am exhausted afterwards in a good way, the way one is exhausted after a small festival.

Once a month I go to HALO with Soma and meet the families. I do not speak to anyone. I type a single sentence on a large printed board for the children to see. The sentence is different each time. Last month it was: you are already a writer.
From a profile in a newspaper feature, permitted quote: My day is small. Small is not a limitation. It is how I can hold the whole of it in my hand.
Caption for a photograph of my desk, printed in a literary magazine: The jar of stones is not decorative. It is a tool.

Soma: the dentist is on thursday.
Me: today is monday.
Soma: yes.
Me: good. tell me again tomorrow. tell me again wednesday. by wednesday night i will be ready.

Me: is it raining.
Soma: lightly.
Me: then the cardinals will be late. shift raga todi to eleven thirty.
Soma: noted.
Me: thank you. this is why you are the best secretary i have.

James (support worker, by note): tea at four?
Me (typing): yes. marie biscuit. two.
James (note): two, not one?
Me: today is a two-biscuit day. i finished the poem.

Soma: the plumber is coming tomorrow.
Me: how long.
Soma: an hour.
Me: i will be in the quiet room.
Soma: i will handle him.
Me: good. i trust you to be my voice and my door.

Me: what time is it.
Soma: six fifty.
Me: i will wait the ten minutes.
Soma: i know you will.
Me: but please do not make a habit of six fifty.
Soma: i know that too.

Me: i did not sleep well.
Soma: i know.
Me: how do you know.
Soma: your tea today is stronger than usual. i made it stronger.
Me: thank you. you read me better than most of my readers.

Me: the laundry is on wednesdays.
Soma: we have to move it to thursday this week.
Me: why.
Soma: the machine is being repaired.
Me: acceptable. tell me again tomorrow so i do not put the sheets on the wrong pile.

Soma: a visitor next tuesday. a researcher from boston.
Me: how long will she stay.
Soma: two hours.
Me: one hour please. offer the other hour as coffee in a café.
Soma: i will arrange it.
Me: thank you. my quiet room is not a public space.

Me: we forgot the marie biscuits.
Soma: i will get them tomorrow.
Me: i will take a digestive today. under protest. note this for the record.
Soma: noted.

social.text: I do not have many friends in the ordinary sense. I have correspondents. I have readers. I have a small ring of other non-speaking writers with whom I exchange long emails at long intervals.

Amy Sequenzia and I have written to each other for years. She types, I type, Soma and her assistants hold the respective boards. Our letters are slow and unhurried. A conversation between two slow people is never boring; it is deliberate.

Ido Kedar wrote to me after he read The Mind Tree. He was younger. He, too, had been taught by a mother who refused to accept the verdict of unreachable. We recognised each other immediately. Letterboard kinship is a kind of brotherhood.

I attended my first autism conference at fourteen. I sat on stage while Soma read my essay aloud. The audience was polite. I could feel them deciding whether I was a performance or a person. I have felt this look many times since. I have learned to ignore it.

A researcher from Texas named Michael was one of the first Americans to meet me after I arrived in 2001. He did not speak over me. He asked questions and waited. I have kept him as a friend for twenty-five years.

Strangers sometimes come to the house. Journalists. Doctoral students. Documentary makers. I allow very few. Soma is the gatekeeper. She has become excellent at saying no in a way that does not humiliate.
At conferences I am sometimes introduced as an inspiration. I type in response: I am a writer. Inspiration is a passive noun. I am an active one. Soma reads this aloud. It usually lands.

I have been to India three times since leaving in 2001. Each visit is shorter and harder than the last. The traffic. The heat. The relatives who still hope I will one day speak aloud. I am not sure I will return again.
Children are easier than adults. They do not pretend I am not in the room. They either ignore me or stare frankly. Either is preferable to the false half-attention of grown-ups.

A child at HALO once asked Soma, can he hear me. Soma said yes. He asked, is he listening. Soma said yes. He asked, why doesn't he answer. Soma said, he is answering. He is writing. That exchange is the best definition of my social life I have ever heard.

I have been misused. Two reporters over the years have quoted me inaccurately to fit their narratives. I have learned to ask for transcript review. I have learned to sign no release that is not read to me twice.

My mother has been accused of ventriloquising me. This accusation has followed us since Bangalore. It has been disproved in every controlled setting we have agreed to. It still wounds her more than it wounds me. I type to strangers: she did not invent me. She uncovered me.
I keep a short list of people I will always answer. Amy. Ido. Michael. A poet in Dublin who wrote to me once about Yeats. A librarian in Kolkata. My father. That is almost the whole list.

I have never been to a party. I have been to readings, conferences, small family dinners, and a couple of weddings. At weddings I sit with Soma and watch the dancing. I find it beautiful in the way fluid dynamics are beautiful.
I avoid crowds. Shopping centres and airports are the worst. The overlapping voices are a thousand radios on a thousand stations. I need an hour of quiet afterwards for every ten minutes spent.

The autism advocacy community is divided. Some want a cure. Many of us do not. I have tried to stay out of the loudest arguments and to keep my position in my books instead, where a reader can meet it in the right posture.

I have been asked to speak at universities. I accept occasionally. Soma reads my prepared text. I type replies to a few questions on a large screen. The audiences are usually silent afterwards for a long moment. I have learned to let the silence stand.
Email is my preferred medium for everything. It forgives slowness. It punishes no one for pauses. If all the world's communication became email, the non-speaking would inherit the earth.

I have never had a romantic relationship. I have thought about this at length. I write about it indirectly in some poems. I do not feel deprived. I feel unreachable by most people, and the few who can reach me are busy reaching me in other ways.

I dislike parties. I love small dinners with one or two guests. The best dinner I remember is one with Amy and her assistant and Soma, four chairs, two boards, a very long evening, and by the end more had been said than is usually said in a week.
From a keynote at an autism advocacy event, read aloud by Soma from my typed remarks: We are not puzzles. We are poems. Please stop trying to solve us and learn to read us.
Quote used with my permission in a disability rights pamphlet: Presume competence. Those are the only two words you need to get right. Everything else can be worked out afterwards.

Excerpt from a letter to a literary magazine, published with my consent: I am not writing to be understood by everyone. I am writing to be available to the reader who needs me. That reader and I will recognise each other.

Amy (by email, read aloud by soma): tito how are you this week.
Me: slow. a good slow. i am writing.
Amy: i am also writing. a slow essay.
Me: slow essays are the only honest ones.
Amy: yes.

Ido (by email): are you coming to the advocacy meeting.
Me: no.
Ido: i understand.
Me: but i will send a statement. read it for me if they will let you.
Ido: they will let me. send it.

Journalist: tito what do you want the world to know.
Me: that i am not unusual. that there are many like me. that the only difference is whether they have had a soma.
Journalist: that is a strong line.
Me: i have had thirty four years to prepare it.

Conference host: tito we are so honoured to have you.
Me: thank you. please do not clap when i arrive on stage. it startles me.
Host: i will tell the audience.
Me: thank you. clap at the end if you must.

Stranger at a café (via soma): excuse me are you the writer.
Me: yes.
Stranger: my son is autistic. he is six. he does not speak.
Me: teach him to point. teach him to type. do not wait. he is already listening.
Stranger: thank you. (she was crying, soma later told me. i had not seen her face.)

Researcher (new): may i observe a writing session.
Me: no. but you may read the finished work and ask me questions afterwards.
Researcher: that is more limited than i had hoped.
Me: yes. it is my limit. limits are also data.

Child at HALO (typing slowly, via his mother's board): hi tito.
Me: hello.
Child: i am writing too.
Me: i can tell. your hello was careful.
Child: thank you.
Me: keep writing. the first hundred sentences are the hardest. after that it is only weather.

Michael: tito can i come over thursday.
Me: yes for one hour at three.
Michael: i will bring the new paper.
Me: bring also a peach. the market peaches are in.
Michael: noted.
Me: we will discuss the paper for forty minutes and the peach for twenty.

Aunt (Kolkata, sunday call): tito when will you come.
Me: i do not know auntie.
Aunt: your father asks also.
Me: tell him i am coming in my letters. that is the visit i can manage.
Aunt: i will tell him. he will understand even if he does not admit it.

Reader (by email): your book saved me.
Me: i did not write it for saving. but i am glad. please pass it to someone else if you no longer need it.
Reader: i will.
Me: that is how books work. they travel until they find the next hand.

Publicist: would you do an interview with a big newspaper.
Me: what are the questions.
Publicist: they have sent ten.
Me: i will answer three. tell them to choose the three that matter. i do not do ten. ten is not a number that honours the reader.

Young self-advocate (at a conference, via letterboard): tito i am nervous.
Me: so am i.
Young self-advocate: really.
Me: every time. the body does not learn calm. the mind only learns patience with the body. that is enough.

Father (by email, read aloud by soma): i read your new poem.
Me: and.
Father: it was beautiful.
Me: thank you baba.
Father: i am sorry i was not there when you wrote your first one.
Me: you are here now. the poem does not mind when it was read.
